metadata.title: Claude, In Your Hands — bot vs bot, season one · 7/7
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity UI beats; exhibits keep their own look (MUSINIQUE.md graded-exhibit law).
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: Hawaiian (one-word budget — Musinique persona). Wagwan stays Bear's.
metadata.charter: MUSINIQUE.md — fights for the indie musician; receipts law; roll-your-own law; no fake numbers
metadata.note: ILLUSTRATE LAW: UI at B00 (with RESULT lines) / verdict / handoff / outro only; each inner beat carries an illustration spec in props.illustration (kind: concept | pattern | code | exhibit) — build per medhavy_com/youtube/mcp/remotion-src worked example + skills/animated-deck patterns. ROLL-YOUR-OWN LAW: the handoff paste is a deck piece the viewer keeps. RECEIPTS LAW: claims trace to RECEIPTS.md beside the playlist; illustrative examples labeled as such.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The finale says six prompts and zero subscriptions gave me a deck I own as text files in my own folder, and I don't want to lose that. Help me lock it in: (1) lay out the folder structure that holds all six prompts plus my voice and catalog files so no platform can sunset them; (2) write the one-line review-gate check that enforces the four things that never ride a schedule; (3) show me the honest ledger math comparing my minutes of review against the tabs and dead placement fees I stopped paying.

props.command: The finale says six prompts and zero subscriptions gave me a deck I own as text files in my own folder, and I don't want to lose that. Help me lock it in: (1) lay out the folder structure that holds all six prompts plus my voice and catalog files so no platform can sunset them; (2) write the one-line review-gate check that enforces the four things that never ride a schedule; (3) show me the honest ledger math comparing my minutes of review against the tabs and dead placement fees I stopped paying.
output: the machine drafts, hunts, reads, files
you say what's true, spend money, sign your name

beats.narration_text: The arsenal check. Episode one: your work, sorted into lanes. Two: the recipes on schedule. Three: the source gate that catches the costume. Four: your voice in a file. Five: the catalog mirror with receipts. Six: the pitch machine, drafts only. Six prompts. Zero subscriptions to promise-sellers. That's the deck — and you rolled it yourself.

illustration.spec: The full promo road case opens: six pieces slot in — lane-sorter (E1), Monday digest (E2), source gate (E3), voice file (E4), catalog mirror (E5), pitch machine (E6)

beats.narration_text: Now the line, permanently. Four things never ride any schedule. Claims of fact — a number goes out verified or it doesn't go out. Money — no machine spends yours. Your name — no named human gets machine-sent mail signed you. And your taste — the thing the whole catalog serves. Everything else is negotiable labor. These four are you.

illustration.spec: The red-line card, poster-plain: claims of fact · money · your name · your taste — each with a hand icon

beats.narration_text: Here's the season's thesis with the receipts in. The platforms aim machines at your attention. The scammers aim machines at your hope. This season aimed machines at your grind — and the difference between the three is never the machinery. It's who owns it, and whose interest it serves. Yours now exists. It serves you.

illustration.spec: two indies, split at 'who owns the machine': rents promises -> money out, receipts never; rolls their own -> hours back, receipts always

beats.narration_text: The honest ledger, one last time, because we don't sell here. It can't hear your mix. Schedules need the laptop awake. Agentic runs cost more than chat. Reviewing drafts costs you minutes every day. Add it all up — then price it against the week of research tabs, the three-hundred-dollar placement that never came, and the evenings you got back. The ledger isn't close.

illustration.spec: Honest-ledger card: can't hear audio · desktop awake for schedules · agentic runs cost usage · drafts need review time — each priced against 'a week of tabs' and '$300 placements'

beats.narration_text: One more thing, and it's the quiet one. Every piece of this deck is a text file in a folder you own. No platform can sunset it. No service can raise the price. The promo industry's whole model is renting you tools with promises attached. You just ended your lease.

illustration.spec: Two cards: 'their promise' (recurring charge, no receipts) torn up; 'your deck' (six prompt files in a folder) kept

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. One page, one season. The machine drafts, hunts, reads, and files. You say what's true, spend the money, sign your name, and keep the taste. That's not a compromise with the tools. That's the whole point of them.

props.artifactTitle: Claude, In Your Hands — one page
artifactLines: The deck: lane-sorter · Monday digest · source gate · voice file · catalog mirror · pitch machine — six files, yours.
The red lines: facts verified, money human-spent, your name human-signed, your taste untouched.
The thesis: bot vs bot — the machinery is neutral; ownership isn't. Yours serves you now.
The ledger: real costs, honestly stated — and not close against the grind it replaces.

beats.narration_text: Your turn, one last time. Paste this and let it grade your own deck against the season. What comes back missing is your homework. What comes back complete is yours — no lease, no landlord. Go make the record.

props.command: Audit my deck: check my project for the six pieces from this season — lane sorting, the Monday digest, the source-tier gate, my brand-voice file, the catalog read, the pitch recipe. List what's missing or stale, and draft the maintenance schedule: what I review daily (drafts), weekly (digest), and quarterly (voice file, source tiers).

beats.narration_text: Claude, In Your Hands — bot vs bot, season one · 7/7
